1: If you receive my words and treasure up my commandments — wisdom as covenant gift to be received and stored. The image of treasuring recalls Jesus's parable of the treasure hidden in a field (Matthew 13:44): the one who discovers wisdom sells everything to obtain it. More directly, the covenant instruction to 'bind these words on your heart' (Deuteronomy 6:6) finds its christological fulfillment in Christ's teaching that enters not just the ear but transforms the heart (Hebrews 8:10: I will put my laws into their minds and write them on their hearts).
2: Making your ear attentive to wisdom and inclining your heart to understanding — the disposition of receptive faith. Paul's principle that 'faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ' (Romans 10:17) is the NT application of this posture: attentiveness to the word is the mechanism of wisdom's reception. Wisdom is not achieved by intellectual effort alone but received by attentive openness — precisely the posture Mary models when she 'sat at the Lord's feet and listened to his teaching' (Luke 10:39).
3: If you call out for insight and raise your voice for understanding — the seeking that precedes finding. Jesus's invitation in Matthew 7:7-8 ('ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you') directly echoes the Proverbs pattern of wisdom obtained through earnest seeking. The difference in the NT is that the one who is sought has now become visible: seeking wisdom leads to Christ, who is wisdom (Colossians 2:3).
4: If you seek it like silver and search for it as for hidden treasures — wisdom's worth exceeding material wealth. The parable of the pearl of great price (Matthew 13:45-46) makes the same comparison explicit: the merchant sold everything he had to purchase the one pearl of surpassing value. Christ as wisdom is the hidden treasure and the pearl of great price; the seeking that Proverbs mandates is the seeking of the kingdom, which Christ calls his followers to pursue above all else (Matthew 6:33).
5: Then you will understand the fear of the LORD and find the knowledge of God — the goal of wisdom-seeking is personal knowledge of YHWH. This is the new covenant's defining promise: 'They shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest' (Jeremiah 31:34; Hebrews 8:11). Jesus makes it explicit: 'This is eternal life, that they know you the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent' (John 17:3). The knowledge of God that Proverbs 2:5 holds out as wisdom's goal is given in Christ.
6: For the LORD gives wisdom; from his mouth come knowledge and understanding — wisdom as divine gift, not human achievement. James 1:5 cites exactly this principle: 'If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him.' The christological dimension: Christ himself is the word from God's mouth (John 1:1-14), the fullness of wisdom from the source of all wisdom. Asking for wisdom means asking to know Christ more fully.
7: He stores up sound wisdom for the upright; he is a shield to those who walk in integrity — the storing and shielding function of divine wisdom. The image of God as shield (Psalms 18:2; Proverbs 28:7) is applied in the NT to faith: 'the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one' (Ephesians 6:16). The stored-up wisdom of God is disclosed in Christ, and the protection of the upright is secured through the one who bore every dart on the cross.
8: Guarding the paths of justice and watching over the way of his saints — divine providence over the covenant community. The NT presents Christ as the shepherd who guards his sheep: 'I am the good shepherd; I know my own and my own know me' (John 10:14). The guarding of the just in Proverbs 2:8 is a revelation of God's character — his protective oversight of those who walk with him — fulfilled in Christ's constant intercession (Hebrews 7:25: always living to make intercession) and his promise never to lose those given to him (John 10:28-29).
9: Then you will understand righteousness and justice and equity, every good path — moral comprehension that comes from wisdom's formation. Paul identifies Christ as the one who is 'our righteousness' (1 Corinthians 1:30): the moral formation that wisdom offers is not merely ethical instruction but participation in the character of Christ. 'Understanding every good path' is the language of Micah 6:8 (justice, mercy, humility) reexpressed in the NT as conformity to Christ's image (Romans 8:29).
10: When wisdom enters your heart and knowledge is pleasant to your soul — wisdom's internalization is the new covenant's distinctive promise. Jeremiah 31:33 (the new covenant): 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts.' Ezekiel 36:26-27: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you.' Proverbs 2:10 anticipates what the new covenant effects: wisdom is no longer external instruction but an internalized reality, made possible by the Spirit of Christ who dwells in believers (Romans 8:9-11).
11: Discretion will guard you; understanding will watch over you — the double protection of internalized wisdom. The Holy Spirit is described in similar terms: the Paraclete (advocate, helper, guard) who will 'teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you' (John 14:26). The Spirit indwells and guards; wisdom internalized in the NT is inseparable from the Spirit's presence, the deposit and guarantee of the inheritance (Ephesians 1:13-14).
12: Delivering you from the way of evil, from men of perverted speech — wisdom as deliverance from the power of evil. The NT extends this: 'the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials' (2 Peter 2:9). More fundamentally, Christ himself is the deliverer — 'he has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son' (Colossians 1:13). The deliverance from evil paths that wisdom offers is a shadow of the redemption Christ accomplishes.
13: Men who forsake the paths of uprightness to walk in the ways of darkness — apostasy and moral inversion. The NT warning against forsaking the assembly (Hebrews 10:25) and the repeated call not to return to the 'old self' (Ephesians 4:22) and not to drift away from the message heard (Hebrews 2:1) engage this theme. The one who delivers from darkness is Christ, who has come as 'the light of the world' (John 8:12), and those who forsake uprightness return from light to darkness.
14: Who rejoice in doing evil and delight in the perverseness of evil — the characterization of depravity as inversion of delight. Romans 1:32 uses this same diagnostic: the wicked 'not only do them but give approval to those who practice them.' The unredeemed rejoice in evil; the redeemed are called to a different delight — 'Rejoice in the Lord always' (Philippians 4:4). The transformation of delight, from evil to the Lord, is what wisdom and ultimately what Christ effects in the life of the disciple.
15: Men whose paths are crooked, and who are devious in their ways — moral crookedness as the mark of the fool. The OT preparation involves making crooked paths straight (Isaiah 40:3-4), and this is applied to John the Baptist's ministry (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4-5) as preparation for Christ's coming. Crooked paths are what Christ came to straighten; his ministry — healing, forgiving, calling — is the making-straight of what was bent.
16: Delivering you from the forbidden woman — the covenant-fidelity dimension of wisdom. The adulteress who lures away from the covenant is a metaphor that deepens in the NT: Paul uses marriage imagery to describe the exclusive covenant relationship between Christ and the church (2 Corinthians 11:2: 'I feel a divine jealousy for you, since I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ'). Departure from wisdom is infidelity to Christ; wisdom's deliverance is faithfulness to the covenant.
17: Who forsakes the companion of her youth and forgets the covenant of her God — the adulteress as covenant-breaker. The 'covenant of God' is the marriage covenant, but the OT consistently uses marriage as a figure for the YHWH-Israel covenant (Hosea 2:2-13; Jeremiah 2:2; Ezek 16). The NT applies the bride-bridegroom image to Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:25-32; Revelation 19:7-9). Forgetting the covenant of God is, in the fullest sense, forgetting Christ — and wisdom delivers from that spiritual adultery.
18: Her house sinks down to death and her paths to the shades — the way of folly leads ultimately to Sheol. The NT's confrontation with death is christological: Christ 'tasted death for everyone' (Hebrews 2:9) and by his death destroyed 'the one who has the power of death' (Hebrews 2:14). The sinking house of folly is what Sheol represents; the resurrection is the inversion — from sinking into death to rising into life. Wisdom's path avoids the downward trajectory that Christ descended into and overcame.
19: None who go to her come back, nor do they regain the paths of life — the irreversibility of the way of folly without redemption. The prodigal son who 'came to himself' (Luke 15:17) and returned is the NT's answer to this verse's despair: there is a return possible, but only through repentance and the father's reception. Christ's parable directly addresses the possibility of return for those who have walked the way of folly — something wisdom alone cannot provide; it requires the Father's running out to meet the returning son.
20: Walk in the way of the good and keep to the paths of the righteous — the positive call of wisdom after the negative warnings. Jesus declares himself 'the way' (John 14:6): the way of the good is not merely a moral standard but a person. Walking in the way of wisdom becomes, in the NT, following Christ — the explicit language of discipleship ('follow me,' Mark 1:17) replaces wisdom's abstract path-walking with attachment to a person.
21: For the upright will inhabit the land, and those with integrity will remain in it — covenant inheritance tied to covenant fidelity. The Beatitude — 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5) — is a direct engagement with this Proverbs theme (and Psalms 37:11). Christ declares that the inheritance is given not to the violent but to the meek; wisdom's path of integrity leads to the inheritance that the NT presents as the new creation, the renewed earth.
22: But the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the treacherous will be rooted out of it — the eschatological removal of evil. The parable of the wheat and tares (Matthew 13:36-43) presents the same two destinies at the harvest: the tares are bound and burned; the wheat enters the kingdom. The 'cutting off from the land' in Proverbs 2:22 is the OT form of what the NT presents as the final judgment — the separation of the wicked from the inheritance of the righteous in Christ.
